title: Raclette
subtitle: melted Alpine cheese over waxy potatoes with cornichons and charcuterie
excerpt: Raclette is a half-wheel of cheese melted by a heat source and scraped directly onto boiled waxy potatoes. With cornichons, pickled onions, and sliced charcuterie alongside, it is the simplest and most satisfying winter table you can set. Assembly rather than cooking; the cheese does the work.
sourceNotes:
Philéas Gilbert and Emile Fetu, Le Livre de Cuisine (1900). Raclette as a preparation of melted cheese with potatoes, documented in the Savoyard tradition. Public domain.

Note: there is no raclette cheese slug in the master ingredient lookup. The slug gruyere (Gruyère) is used as the closest available Alpine melting cheese. A dedicated raclette ingredient entry would improve accuracy. Raclette cheese and Gruyère are both washed-rind Alpine cheeses with similar melting behaviour.

glossaryTerms.definition: Both a type of Alpine cheese and the dish made with it. The word comes from the French 'racler', to scrape. The cheese is melted under a heat source and scraped directly onto the plate. The dish is traditional in the Savoie and Valais regions on the border of France and Switzerland.

content.text: Raclette is named for the action of making it: the word comes from 'racler', to scrape.

cheese is melted by heat until its surface bubbles and browns, then scraped onto a plate of hot waxy potatoes. Add cornichons, pickled onions, and cold sliced charcuterie, and you have a complete supper that requires almost no cooking skill at all.

The cheese is the key purchase. Raclette cheese is available from most good cheese shops and some supermarkets, sold in slices or half-wheels. If you cannot find it, a young Gruyère will melt in the same way and carries a similar nutty, creamy flavour. What you need is an Alpine cheese with good melting behaviour; anything too aged or too hard will not run properly. Under a hot grill, a thick slice takes 3 to 4 minutes to bubble and colour at the edges.

items.prepNote: raclette cheese preferred; Gruyère is the closest substitute. Cut into thick slices or half-rounds about 1 cm thick
or any combination of thin-sliced ham, salami, or air-dried beef

content.text: The traditional way to melt raclette is with a dedicated raclette machine: an electric heating element with small trays that hold the cheese slices. These are widely available and make the whole process very relaxed. Without one, a domestic grill set to high works well. Arrange the cheese on a foil-lined grill tray and let each person's portion bubble and colour before scraping it off directly at the table.

Put the {{potato}} potatoes in a large saucepan of cold salted water. Bring to the boil and cook for 18 to 22 minutes until tender when pierced with a knife. Do not peel them; waxy potatoes hold together best in their skins. Drain and keep warm in the pot with the lid on.

Heat your grill to its highest setting. Lay the {{gruyere}} of cheese on a foil-lined tray in a single layer. Place under the grill for 3 to 5 minutes until the surface bubbles and the edges begin to colour. Watch it closely; the line between perfectly melted and overcooked is narrow. As soon as the surface is liquid and starting to brown, it is ready.

Set each potato on a warm plate and press it lightly to crack the skin open. Scrape the melted cheese over the potatoes using a flat knife or spatula. Add the {{cornichons}} cornichons to the plate alongside the cold charcuterie. Season with black pepper. Work in rounds so everyone gets hot cheese; the table becomes the kitchen.

items.symptom: Cheese does not melt smoothly, stays rubbery
items.cause: Cheese too cold from the fridge, or heat not high enough
items.fix: Bring the cheese to room temperature for 30 minutes before grilling; use your grill's highest setting and position the tray close to the element

items.symptom: Cheese bubbles and separates into oil and solids
items.cause: Heat too aggressive, or cheese too aged
items.fix: A very aged Gruyère will split under high heat; use a younger cheese and reduce the grill time slightly

items.cause: Overcooked or not drained and dried properly
items.fix: Drain the potatoes and return them briefly to the hot empty pan over low heat to evaporate excess moisture before serving

items.symptom: Cheese cools and stiffens before it can be scraped
items.cause: Working too slowly, or plates cold
items.fix: Warm the plates before serving; scrape and serve immediately once the cheese comes out from under the grill

content.text: Raclette belongs to the border country between France and Switzerland: the Savoie, Haute-Savoie, and the Swiss canton of Valais. The oldest accounts describe the cheese being melted against a fire on a stone, then scraped onto bread or potatoes by shepherds in the high pastures. The electric raclette machine appeared in the mid-twentieth century and brought the dish into domestic kitchens throughout the region. Today it is the centrepiece of the winter social table: long, slow, everyone feeding themselves from the same machine, conversation running as long as the cheese holds out.
